case_name: Ajay Alias Vishal Veeru Devgan vs The Artists Planet & Ors
court: High Court of Delhi at New Delhi
facts: The Plaintiff, Ajay alias Vishal Veeru Devgan, an Indian film actor, filed CS(COMM) 1269/2025 seeking permanent injunctions for trademark infringement, passing off, copyright infringement, violation of personality/publicity rights and related reliefs against multiple defendants including marketplaces, social media pages, platform intermediaries and unknown 'John Doe' uploaders. The suit complained of unauthorized sale of merchandise, impersonation, and AI-generated deepfake and pornographic face-morphed content accessible via specific URLs and social media accounts. The Plaintiff sought ex-parte ad-interim injunctions, takedown/delisting of identified URLs/products, blocking of domains by ISPs and disclosure of Basic Subscriber Information (BSI) to identify unknown uploaders. The Court conducted an interlocutory hearing and made multiple interim directions (takedown, delisting, domain locking and timelines) while ordering service/filing directions and compliance affidavits.
arguments_claimant: The Plaintiff argued that he is a nationally celebrated public figure whose name, image, voice and likeness have immense goodwill and commercial value and that unauthorized merchandising, impersonation and AI-generated deepfakes/pornographic morphed content unlawfully exploit his persona and cause irreparable harm. He sought injunctions, takedown/delisting of infringing URLs and products, blocking of offending domains/websites, BSI disclosure to identify John Doe uploaders, and argued that platforms' 'AI training' or third-party policies multiply the harm by enabling reuse and monetisation of infringing content. The Plaintiff relied on trademark registrations, documents evidencing social media reach and MeitY SOP material and sought exemption from pre-institution mediation on urgency grounds.
arguments_respondent: Defendants (marketplaces and intermediaries) said they will abide by interim directions and asked that takedown orders be first directed to uploaders and, if uploaders fail, platforms take down content; some intermediaries sought clarification that ISPs may block domains but not specific URLs. Defendant No. 7 and others (platforms) offered to comply with court directions but highlighted procedural limits and insisted on following statutory grievance/intermediary mechanisms; proforma defendants sought time and clarified scope of blocking powers. The government respondents (MeiTY/DoT) appearing as proforma defendants raised that they are proforma and do not oppose certain exemptions or directions.
ratio_decidendi: The Court held that "exemption from the requirement of pre- institution mediation is granted to the Plaintiff" having regard to urgency and "in light of the judgement of the Supreme Court in Yamini Manohar v. T.K.D. Keerthi". The Court found prima facie that the Plaintiff is a well-known public figure and that "prima facie, the Plaintiff's personality traits and/or parts thereof, including the Plaintiff's name, likeness, voice, and image are protectable elements of the Plaintiff's personality rights" and therefore entitled to seek injunctions against unauthorised commercial exploitation. The Court qualified the relief by emphasising statutory remedies for online intermediaries, noting that some complained-of posts "would have been liable to be actioned as per Rule 3(2) of the IT Intermediary Rules" and warning that "if henceforth a complainant/plaintiff approaches the Court without availing the statutory mechanism provided under the IT Intermediary Rules, the said party may be held to be not entitled to an ex-parte ad-interim injunction." The balance of convenience and risk of irreparable injury justified interim relief (takedown/delisting and blocking directions) pending fuller adjudication.
final_order: The Court granted ex-parte ad-interim injunctions restraining identified defendants (Nos. 1,2,6,7,8,9,10,11 and John Doe entities) from exploiting the Plaintiff's name/image/voice/likeness; directed specific takedown/delisting of URLs and products in Annexure A, domain locking/suspension for Defendant No.1 if it fails to act, and ordered compliance within 72 hours; directed intermediaries to provide BSI within four weeks and to file compliance affidavits within four weeks. Notices were issued to non-appearing defendants, timelines set for pleadings/service and the matter listed before the Joint Registrar and Court.
summary: This is an interlocutory Delhi High Court order in a celebrity personality-rights suit by Ajay Devgn against multiple marketplaces, social media pages, platform intermediaries and unknown uploaders for unauthorised merchandise and AI-generated deepfakes/pornographic morphed images. The Court granted interim reliefs: ex-parte ad-interim injunctions, ordered takedown/delisting of specified URLs/products, domain-lock direction and BSI disclosure, while noting complainants should normally use the IT Intermediary Rules grievance mechanism before seeking ex-parte relief. The order exempts the Plaintiff from pre-institution mediation due to urgency and instructs defendants and proforma intermediaries to comply within fixed timelines.
embedding_summary: Ajay Alias Vishal Veeru Devgan v. The Artists Planet & Ors, High Court of Delhi, 27 November 2025, CS(COMM) 1269/2025. Plaintiff (celebrity actor Ajay Devgn) sued marketplaces (The Artists Planet, Good Hope, Amazon, Redbubble, Meesho), social media pages (Facebook pages, YouTube channels), platform intermediaries (Google/YouTube, Meta Platforms, X Corp.), domain registrar and ISPs and unknown John Does for unauthorised merchandise sales, impersonation, AI-generated deepfakes and pornographic face-morphed content accessible via listed URLs. Key legal issues: protectability of personality/publicity rights, jurisdiction for interim injunctions, interplay with IT Intermediary Rules 2021 (Rule 3(2)), requirement to avail statutory grievance mechanism, disclosure of Basic Subscriber Information (BSI), domain blocking powers and exemption from pre-institution mediation under Section 12A Commercial Courts Act (citing Yamini Manohar v. T.K.D. Keerthi). Outcome: Court granted interim ex-parte injunctions, ordered takedown/delisting and domain-locking directions with compliance timelines (72 hours for takedown; BSI/compliance affidavits within 4 weeks), issued notices to non-appearing defendants and emphasised that failure to use statutory intermediary grievance remedies may bar ex-parte relief. No monetary compensation awarded.
legal_principles: Prima facie protectability of celebrity personality/publicity rights (name, likeness, voice, image) to seek injunctions against unauthorised commercial exploitation

Exemption from pre-institution mediation is permissible where the suit contemplates urgent interim relief ("exemption from the requirement of pre- institution mediation is granted" citing Yamini Manohar)
Complainants should normally avail the statutory IT Intermediary grievance mechanism (Rule 3(2)) before seeking ex-parte ad-interim injunctions; failure to do so may preclude such relief
Intermediaries/platforms must provide Basic Subscriber Information (BSI) for identification of John Doe uploaders when directed by the Court
Court-ordered takedown/delisting and domain-locking/ISP blocking are available interim remedies where balance of convenience and irreparable injury are established
Proforma intermediaries/platforms are obliged to act promptly (within specified timelines) to remove or delist mirror/identical infringing content and file compliance affidavits
sections_cited: Section 80(2) r/w Section 151 Code of Civil Procedure, 1908
Section 63(4)(c) of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023
Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015
Order XI Rule 1(4) CPC [as amended by the Commercial Courts Act, 2015]
The Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021 (Rule 3(2))
Sections 38, 38A and 38B of the Copyright Act, 1957
cases_cited: D.M. Entertainment vs Baby Gift House
Anil Kapoor Vs. Simply Life India & Ors.
Jaikishan Kaku Bhai Sarf Alias Jackie Shroff Vs. The Peppy Store and Ors.
bm25_text: Ajay Alias Vishal Veeru Devgan vs The Artists Planet & Ors High Court of Delhi at New Delhi 2025 The Plaintiff, Ajay alias Vishal Veeru Devgan, an Indian film actor, filed CS(COMM) 1269/2025 seeking permanent injunctions for trademark infringement, passing off, copyright infringement, violation of personality/publicity rights and related reliefs against multiple defendants including marketplaces, social media pages, platform intermediaries and unknown 'John Doe' uploaders. The suit complained of unauthorized sale of merchandise, impersonation, and AI-generated deepfake and pornographic face-morphed content accessible via specific URLs and social media accounts. The Plaintiff sought ex-parte ad-interim injunctions, takedown/delisting of identified URLs/products, blocking of domains by ISPs and disclosure of Basic Subscriber Information (BSI) to identify unknown uploaders. The Court conducted an interlocutory hearing and made multiple interim directions (takedown, delisting, domain locking and timelines) while ordering service/filing directions and compliance affidavits. The Plaintiff argued that he is a nationally celebrated public figure whose name, image, voice and likeness have immense goodwill and commercial value and that unauthorized merchandising, impersonation and AI-generated deepfakes/pornographic morphed content unlawfully exploit his persona and cause irreparable harm. He sought injunctions, takedown/delisting of infringing URLs and products, blocking of offending domains/websites, BSI disclosure to identify John Doe uploaders, and argued that platforms' 'AI training' or third-party policies multiply the harm by enabling reuse and monetisation of infringing content. The Plaintiff relied on trademark registrations, documents evidencing social media reach and MeitY SOP material and sought exemption from pre-institution mediation on urgency grounds. Defendants (marketplaces and intermediaries) said they will abide by interim directions and asked that takedown orders be first directed to uploaders and, if uploaders fail, platforms take down content; some intermediaries sought clarification that ISPs may block domains but not specific URLs. Defendant No. 7 and others (platforms) offered to comply with court directions but highlighted procedural limits and insisted on following statutory grievance/intermediary mechanisms; proforma defendants sought time and clarified scope of blocking powers. The government respondents (MeiTY/DoT) appearing as proforma defendants raised that they are proforma and do not oppose certain exemptions or directions. The Court held that "exemption from the requirement of pre- institution mediation is granted to the Plaintiff" having regard to urgency and "in light of the judgement of the Supreme Court in Yamini Manohar v. T.K.D. Keerthi". The Court found prima facie that the Plaintiff is a well-known public figure and that "prima facie, the Plaintiff's personality traits and/or parts thereof, including the Plaintiff's name, likeness, voice, and image are protectable elements of the Plaintiff's personality rights" and therefore entitled to seek injunctions against unauthorised commercial exploitation. The Court qualified the relief by emphasising statutory remedies for online intermediaries, noting that some complained-of posts "would have been liable to be actioned as per Rule 3(2) of the IT Intermediary Rules" and warning that "if henceforth a complainant/plaintiff approaches the Court without availing the statutory mechanism provided under the IT Intermediary Rules, the said party may be held to be not entitled to an ex-parte ad-interim injunction." The balance of convenience and risk of irreparable injury justified interim relief (takedown/delisting and blocking directions) pending fuller adjudication. The Court granted ex-parte ad-interim injunctions restraining identified defendants (Nos. 1,2,6,7,8,9,10,11 and John Doe entities) from exploiting the Plaintiff's name/image/voice/likeness; directed specific takedown/delisting of URLs and products in Annexure A, domain locking/suspension for Defendant No.1 if it fails to act, and ordered compliance within 72 hours; directed intermediaries to provide BSI within four weeks and to file compliance affidavits within four weeks. Notices were issued to non-appearing defendants, timelines set for pleadings/service and the matter listed before the Joint Registrar and Court.
